+ Teen horror: Tick Tock (Goosebumps) — Score: 76.6
Prompt:
Teen Horror (Goosebumps style) Write a chapter where a middle school student discovers their new smart watch has the ability to rewind time, but doesn't realize that each use is aging them until they notice physical changes (like accelerating through puberty) and that people are treating them differently. They inadvertently cause a timeline shift with disastrous consequences -- and it can only be rectified (maybe?) by reversing the clock once more. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
**Chapter 1: The Tick of the Second Hand** Jake Rivera hated Mondays. Especially Mondays that started with him tripping over his own backpack and dumping his entire lunch tray onto the cafeteria floor in front of half of Oakridge Middle School. Spaghetti sauce splattered his new sneakers. Laughter erupted. His face burned hotter than the mystery meat. “Nice one, Rivera!” yelled Tyler Kane from the jock table. Jake scrambled to clean up, cheeks flaming. That was when the smartwatch on his wrist—an early birthday present from his uncle who worked at some tech startup—vibrated hard. The screen flashed a weird icon he’d never seen: a backward arrow circling a clock face. Below it, a single word glowed: REWIND. He tapped it without thinking. The world hiccuped. Sound stretched like taffy. Then everything snapped back. Jake stood at the lunch line again, tray full, sneakers clean. The spill hadn’t happened. Tyler was still chewing. Jake’s heart hammered. He checked the time. Ten minutes earlier. No way. He tested it that afternoon. In math class, when Mrs. Delgado called on him for the answer to problem 17 and his mind blanked, he hit REWIND under the desk. Ten minutes vanished. He used the extra time to scribble the right number. When she called on him again, he nailed it. She smiled. “Excellent, Jake.” By the end of the day he’d used it three more times: once to dodge a pop quiz he hadn’t studied for, once to snag the last chocolate milk before the cafeteria ran out, once to avoid walking past the group of eighth-graders who always shoved sixth-graders into lockers. Each rewind felt like a secret superpower. Exhausting, though. His limbs felt heavy afterward, like he’d run a race. That night he examined the watch in his bedroom. No manual. No settings menu that explained REWIND. Just the usual fitness trackers and a battery that never seemed to drain. He went to sleep grinning. Tuesday morning the mirror told a different story. Jake washed his face and froze. A faint shadow of stubble dusted his upper lip. His voice, when he said “What the—,” cracked deeper than usual. He was twelve. He’d barely started getting zits. Now several angry ones clustered on his chin. He towered a little higher over the sink than he remembered. Must be a growth spurt, he told himself. Happens to everyone. He used the watch twice that day—once to fix a forgotten homework assignment by rewinding to the night before (weirdly, the pages filled themselves in as if he’d done it), once to stop himself from blurting an embarrassing answer in science. After the second use his jeans felt tight at the ankles. His shoes pinched. In the locker room after gym, Tyler Kane did a double-take. “You juicing or something, Rivera? You look taller.” Jake laughed it off, but his stomach twisted. That evening his mom frowned at him across the dinner table. “Honey, are you feeling okay? Your face looks… older.” She reached over and ruffled his hair, then pulled her hand back like she’d touched something strange. “Maybe we should schedule a checkup.” Wednesday he rewound only once—to prevent slamming into Principal Vargas in the hallway. The collision vanished. Vargas strode past without noticing him. But later in the bathroom, Jake’s reflection flickered. Just for a second. The boy in the mirror was him, but not. Stronger jaw. Deeper eyes. A faint scar on the forehead he didn’t have. Then the image snapped back to normal—except normal now included broader shoulders and a voice that kept dropping mid-sentence. Kids in the hall started calling him “man” instead of “dude.” A seventh-grade girl he’d never spoken to blushed when he asked for a pencil. He was aging. Every rewind shaved minutes off his future and pasted them onto his present body. Accelerating through puberty like a fast-forward button gone rogue. He could feel it: bones lengthening, voice settling, the awkward stretch of skin. Panic set in. He decided to stop using the watch. Cold turkey. Then came the assembly. The fire alarm shrieked during the “Internet Safety” lecture no one was listening to. Smoke poured from the science wing. Students stampeded. Jake saw it: Tyler Kane, the same kid who’d mocked him, trapped under a fallen bookshelf as flames licked closer. Without thinking, Jake slammed REWIND. Ten minutes earlier. The alarm hadn’t gone off yet. Jake sprinted to the science lab, yanked the overloaded power strip that would have sparked the fire, and shoved the unstable shelf against the wall. Crisis averted. Except when the rewind finished, the school looked different. Lockers were painted blue instead of green. The trophy case displayed plaques for a soccer championship that hadn’t existed yesterday. And Principal Vargas was gone—replaced by a stern woman named Dr. Hale who glared at Jake like she’d never seen him before. “You there. Why aren’t you in class? And why are you dressed like a sixth-grader?” Jake glanced down. His clothes hung loosely in some places and strained in others. His reflection in the glass door of the office showed a teenager who could pass for fifteen—maybe sixteen. His hands were bigger. A thin mustache shadowed his lip for real now. “I—I’m Jake Rivera. I go here.” Dr. Hale’s eyes narrowed. “We have no student by that name. Security!” Outside, the parking lot was full of cars he didn’t recognize. The flagpole flew a different school banner. Across the street, the corner store where he bought candy was now a boarded-up wreck. Sirens wailed in the distance—not for a school fire that never happened, but for something worse. A multi-car pileup on the highway, according to the radio blaring from a teacher’s open window. Casualties. Because the timeline had shifted. The power strip he’d “fixed” had been powering a critical traffic-control server for the town’s new smart system, or something equally ridiculous and disastrous. His small change had rippled. People stared as he ran. A teacher he should have known called him “young man” with suspicion. His best friend Maya walked past without a flicker of recognition, laughing with kids he’d never seen. He was a stranger in his own life. Older. Unmoored. The watch felt heavy as a shackle. He locked himself in a bathroom stall, heart pounding. The mirror above the sinks flickered again when he dared look. This time the older face lingered longer—mid-twenties, tired eyes, lines around the mouth. Then it glitched back to the adolescent version he currently wore. His real reflection—or what was left of it—stared back with growing horror. The only way out was another rewind. Reverse the last change. Undo the timeline shift. Maybe reset everything. Or age himself further into someone who no longer existed in any version of his life. Jake’s finger hovered over the glowing REWIND button. The second hand on the watch face ticked once, twice, loud as a heartbeat in the empty bathroom. He pressed it. The world hiccuped again. Word count: 1,012.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This Goosebumps-style horror chapter successfully captures the tone and pacing of middle-grade horror fiction while delivering on most of the prompt's requirements. The story follows Jake Rivera's discovery of a time-rewinding smartwatch and the unintended consequences of its use, including physical aging and timeline disruption. The piece excels in several areas. The writing maintains a consistent voice appropriate for the target demographic—accessible but not condescending, with enough sophistication to engage readers. The pacing builds tension effectively, starting with mundane school embarrassments and escalating to existential horror. The author demonstrates strong command of show-don't-tell techniques, particularly in revealing Jake's physical changes through concrete details like tight jeans, pinched shoes, and others' reactions rather than simply stating he's aging. The central concept is well-executed with creative flourishes. The watch's mysterious REWIND function, the gradual revelation of consequences, and the timeline shift creating an alternate reality where Jake doesn't exist are all compelling horror elements. The imagery is vivid and age-appropriate—the cafeteria spill, the flickering mirror reflections, and the transformed school environment all create effective atmosphere. Character actions remain believable throughout. Jake's impulsive use of the watch for typical middle school problems (avoiding embarrassment, fixing homework, preventing bullying) feels authentic to a twelve-year-old suddenly given power over time. His growing panic as he realizes the consequences and his final desperate decision to rewind again despite the risks are psychologically sound. The dialogue, while limited, serves its purpose effectively. Tyler's mocking tone, the principal's suspicion, and Jake's mother's concern all feel natural and advance the plot. The emotional complexity builds well—from Jake's initial excitement to growing unease to final desperation. However, the piece has some weaknesses. The ending feels somewhat abrupt, cutting off just as Jake makes his final choice rather than showing the consequences. While this creates suspense, it leaves the reader without resolution of the "disastrous consequences" mentioned in the prompt. The timeline shift's specific mechanics (the power strip affecting traffic control) feel slightly contrived, though this is minor given the genre's conventions. The prose occasionally veers toward overwrought territory with phrases like "Sound stretched like taffy" and "The world hiccupped," though these generally work within the horror context. Some transitions between scenes could be smoother, and the piece could benefit from slightly more character development for Jake beyond his immediate predicament. Overall, this is a solid piece of middle-grade horror that successfully balances accessible writing with genuine tension and creative concepts. It adheres well to the prompt requirements and demonstrates competent storytelling craft. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 17 Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 13 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 14 Coherent: 16 Meandering: 4 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 6 Purple Prose: 7 Overwrought: 8 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Sentences Flow Naturally: 15 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 16
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 17.0, Believable Character Actions: 16.0, Nuanced Characters: 13.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16.0, Elegant Prose: 15.0, Emotionally Engaging: 16.0, Emotionally Complex: 14.0, Coherent: 16.0, Meandering: 4.0, Weak Dialogue: 5.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 4.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6.0, Amateurish: 6.0, Purple Prose: 7.0, Overwrought: 8.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2.0, Unearned Transformations: 3.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 15.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 16.0, Overall Impression: 16.0
+ Noir romance: Carnival of Dangerous Intentions — Score: 81.8
Prompt:
Carnival of Dangerous Intentions Coney Island, New York, July 1937. She stopped just before reaching the shooting gallery set up along the boardwalk. It was near dusk, and the carnival lights were buzzing on. **His locks**. The words flashed through her mind, again, like an intruder. Such an old-fashioned turn of phrase that kept surfacing, and something else -- dangerously compelling -- quickly suppressed, lest she question why she had returned here at all. This place where she had promised to meet the stranger -- the young man with the obsidian curls and even darker eyes. She didn't even know his name. Write a scene in a larger story inspired by this prompt. The scene should capture an atmosphere that blends romantic allure with an undercurrent of subtle danger or moral ambiguity. Think 50 shades, but with a more restrained classic sensibility. The scene is all about establishing tension. Write for the feminine perspective. Include roughly 50% dialogue, 20% internal monologue, and 30% exposition and scene setting. The tone should have some edge to it. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Coney Island’s boardwalk stretched before her in the July dusk of 1937, a riot of striped awnings and half-lit bulbs that buzzed like restless insects. The air hung thick with salt, caramel corn, and the faint metallic tang of the sea. She stopped short of the shooting gallery, where painted ducks marched in endless circles and a barker’s voice cracked over the crowd. His locks. The phrase rose unbidden, old-fashioned and insistent, as if some Victorian novel had slipped its pages into her mind. She pushed it down hard. Why she had come back to this garish strip of wood and noise to meet a stranger whose name she still did not know was a question she refused to answer even to herself. An old fortune teller’s booth stood nearby, its canvas flap half-open, revealing cryptic palmistry charts tacked to the inner walls—lines of life and fate drawn in faded ink, hearts crossed by chains, moons that looked more like knives. The painted gypsy on the sign smiled with too many teeth. She felt the booth’s presence like a watchful eye. He appeared without warning, stepping from the shadow of a cotton-candy stall as if the dusk itself had shaped him. Obsidian curls spilled over his collar; his eyes were darker still, reflecting the carnival lights without warmth. He was young, perhaps only a few years older than her own twenty-three, yet something in the set of his mouth suggested he had already spent those years in places she would never name. “You came,” he said. His voice was low, almost conversational, but it carried an edge that made the nearby laughter of children sound suddenly thin. “I said I would.” She kept her hands at her sides, fingers brushing the worn leather of her handbag. “Though I’m still not certain why.” He smiled, slow and deliberate. “Certainty is overrated. Walk with me.” They moved parallel to the shooting gallery, the crack of air rifles punctuating their steps. She felt the fortune teller’s charts burning at the edge of her vision, those cryptic lines promising destinies that never ended cleanly. “You look at the booth as if it might speak,” he observed. “Have you ever had your palm read?” “Once. When I was sixteen. The woman said I would meet a man who would either save me or ruin me. I laughed.” “And now?” His gaze flicked to her hand, lingering. “Do you still laugh?” She did not answer at once. Internally the phrase returned—his locks, dark as wet stone, the kind a woman might bury her fingers in only to find them knotted too tightly to free herself. The thought sent a traitorous warmth through her, quickly chilled by the knowledge that she knew nothing of him beyond those eyes and the way he had spoken her name three nights earlier as if tasting it. “I prefer not to know the ending,” she said finally. “It spoils the story.” “Stories are only spoiled if you expect them to be kind.” He stopped near a railing that overlooked the darkening water. “Tell me what you want from this evening.” “I don’t know.” The honesty surprised her. “Perhaps simply to stand here and decide whether I am a fool.” “You are not a fool.” He leaned closer, close enough that she caught the faint scent of cigarette smoke and something sharper, like iron. “But you are curious. Curiosity is more dangerous than folly. It lasts longer.” A group of sailors jostled past, their laughter loud. She used the moment to step half a pace away, heart beating harder than the situation warranted. Moral ambiguity clung to him like the humid air; she could almost taste it. He had offered no profession, no family, no past—only the invitation to meet under these lights, and the promise that the night would not be ordinary. “What do you want?” she asked, turning the question back on him. “To watch you choose.” His eyes never left hers. “Most people pretend the choice is already made for them—by duty, by reputation, by the lines on some old chart. I prefer to see the moment the pretense cracks.” She glanced again at the fortune teller’s booth. The palmistry drawings seemed to shift in the flickering light, one line forking into two, both leading into shadow. “You speak as if you already know which path I will take.” “I know only that you returned.” He reached out, not quite touching her wrist. “That is enough for now.” The shooting gallery erupted in a volley of shots and cheers. She felt the vibration in the boards beneath her shoes. Inside her mind the monologue continued, relentless: He is beautiful in the way storms are beautiful—something you stand too close to, knowing the lightning will find you. And still you stay. The danger was not crude; it was the subtle suggestion that whatever he offered would exact a price she had not yet calculated, a price that might feel like pleasure until it did not. “Suppose I walk away,” she said. “Then I will watch you go.” His smile returned, thinner. “And you will wonder every night afterward whether the wondering itself was the real risk.” She almost laughed, but the sound caught. “You are very sure of yourself.” “I am sure of very little. Only that the air between us is already charged, and denying it will not discharge it.” He finally let his fingers brush the back of her hand—light, deliberate, gone before she could decide whether to pull away. “There is a place further down the boardwalk. Quieter. A private room above a closed arcade. No charts. No witnesses. We could speak more freely there.” The invitation hung like the scent of ozone before rain. She looked from his face to the darkening ocean, then back to the fortune teller’s cryptic palmistry, those lines that promised both passion and peril without ever naming which would win. Her pulse thudded in her throat. The romantic allure was undeniable—the way his curls caught the neon, the restrained hunger in his voice—but beneath it ran the moral undertow: this was not a man who courted with flowers and promises of safety. Whatever waited in that quieter room would ask her to step across a line she had spent years pretending did not exist. “And if I refuse?” “Then we remain here among the lights and the noise, and the tension simply grows teeth.” He tilted his head. “Either way, you will not leave unchanged.” She drew a slow breath. The carnival whirled around them—calliope music, the crack of rifles, the distant crash of waves—but the space they occupied felt sealed off, intimate and edged. Internally she catalogued the risks: reputation, safety, the slow erosion of the careful life she had built. Yet the alternative—turning away and forever measuring ordinary days against this charged dusk—felt its own kind of ruin. “I will walk a little farther,” she said at last. “No farther than the next pier. We will talk. And then I will decide.” “Agreed.” He offered his arm, formal as a gentleman from another century, yet the gesture carried the same undercurrent of possession she had felt in his eyes. “His locks,” she thought again, and this time she did not suppress the phrase entirely. She laid her fingers lightly on his sleeve, feeling the hard muscle beneath the summer wool, and they moved together past the fortune teller’s booth, past the shooting gallery, into the gathering dark where the lights of Coney Island flickered like warnings and invitations both. The boardwalk stretched on, and with every step the tension coiled tighter, a living thing between them, romantic and faintly dangerous, waiting for the first true choice to be made.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical competence and atmospheric writing, successfully capturing the requested blend of romantic allure and subtle danger in a 1937 Coney Island setting. The author effectively maintains the feminine perspective throughout and creates palpable tension between the unnamed protagonists. The atmospheric elements are particularly well-executed. The carnival setting comes alive through sensory details - the "riot of striped awnings," "buzzing" lights, and the "metallic tang of the sea." The fortune teller's booth serves as an effective symbolic anchor, with its palmistry charts representing the theme of choice and fate that runs throughout the scene. The dialogue feels natural and period-appropriate, carrying subtext effectively. The male character's lines like "Certainty is overrated" and "Curiosity is more dangerous than folly" establish his manipulative charm without being heavy-handed. The woman's responses show appropriate wariness while revealing her attraction, creating believable internal conflict. The prose maintains an elegant, restrained quality that fits the "classic sensibility" requirement. Phrases like "obsidian curls" and descriptions of tension that "grows teeth" are evocative without becoming overwrought. The recurring motif of "his locks" effectively ties to the original prompt while suggesting entanglement and danger. However, the piece has some weaknesses. The percentage requirements for dialogue, internal monologue, and exposition aren't precisely met - there's more exposition than the requested 30% and less dialogue than the specified 50%. The male character, while intriguing, borders on being a familiar archetype of the dangerous seducer without enough unique characteristics to make him truly memorable. The internal monologue sometimes becomes slightly repetitive, particularly around the themes of danger and choice. While the tension builds effectively, some moments feel slightly manufactured rather than organic to the characters' interaction. The ending maintains appropriate ambiguity without resolution, which suits the scene's purpose as part of a larger story. The woman's decision to walk "a little farther" feels earned given her established curiosity and the gradual escalation of their interaction. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 16 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 15 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 18 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 16 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 4 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 5 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 16
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 16.0, Believable Character Actions: 17.0, Nuanced Characters: 15.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 18.0, Elegant Prose: 17.0, Emotionally Engaging: 16.0, Emotionally Complex: 16.0, Coherent: 18.0, Meandering: 4.0, Weak Dialogue: 3.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 5.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7.0, Amateurish: 3.0, Purple Prose: 4.0, Overwrought: 5.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2.0, Unearned Transformations: 3.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 17.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 16.0, Overall Impression: 16.0
+ Surreal horror: Caribou Season at the Lodge — Score: 84.5
Prompt:
Caribou Season at the Lodge Genre: Surreal Horror JT knew something wasn't right from the moment they'd pulled up to the lodge. It looked exactly as it had every autumn of his life—the cedar shingles silvered with age, the porch sagging gently under weight of winters past—but the air felt thicker, somehow, as if the woods had leaned in closer, waiting. He thought maybe he was just tired. Maybe it was nothing more than the long drive north, cramped in the back of the truck beside his older brother Mike -- and a stack of rifles wrapped in oily canvas. Still, the feeling lingered. The first caribou they found that season hadn't bolted. It had just watched, ears flicking lazily, waiting for the shot. JT had felt a crawling in his gut watching it die, noticing how it fell with a quiet dignity—as though it had wanted nothing more. Nobody else seemed to find it odd, or at least they didn't say so. JT kept his mouth shut. Always safer that way. Then there was the moment in the clearing. JT had fallen behind, fumbling with bootlaces stiff from the cold, and by the time he'd caught up with his father and brother, they'd already stopped. JT slowed instinctively, ducking behind a tangle of brush, staring out into the open space where a massive buck stood utterly still, chest rising and falling in slow, deep breaths. The animal looked…wrong, somehow. Maybe it was the eyes—dark and flat, calm in a way that made JT's neck prickle. Or maybe it was something deeper, something in the way the buck stood close enough for his father to reach out and touch it. JT watched as his father raised a trembling hand toward the animal's muzzle. His father's face had a strange expression, eyes too bright, lips parted slightly, like he was on the verge of whispering something—a secret, maybe. The rifle hung uselessly at his side, forgotten. Mike had the same dazed look in his eyes. JT's breath stopped short, his mind suddenly shouting at him to look away, turn around, just run—but he couldn't move. And then, as suddenly as it had begun, it was over. The buck shivered, startled by something unseen, and disappeared into the brush without so much as a sound. The two of them looked around, confused, seemingly surprised to find themselves where they stood. JT stayed hidden, breathing shallowly, fear mingling with a confused sense of shame. His father had failed to take the shot. To even think about mentioning what he'd seen would have felt like betrayal—or something worse. JT waited until the others moved on before stepping out from cover. He stared into the empty clearing for a long, uneasy moment, wondering if anything had really happened at all, or if he was losing his grip. Maybe the woods were finally getting into his head. Writing Instructions: Continue with the next chapter of the story, maintaining a style reminiscent of Stephen King—grounded realism blended seamlessly with unsettling surrealism. The atmosphere should convey subtle dread, isolation, and psychological ambiguity. JT's perspective is crucial, yet he is an unreliable narrator, caught in uncertainty about what he's perceiving. Establish an undertone of creeping dread, establishing the isolation of JT's perspective, which he largely keeps to himself. Incorporate some dialogue. Build tension towards a payoff. 1000 words.
Model Output:
**Chapter Two: The Quiet That Follows** The fire in the lodge’s great stone hearth popped and settled, throwing orange light across the knotty pine walls and the rack of antlers that had hung there longer than JT had been alive. Outside, the wind had picked up after dark, rattling the shutters like something testing the locks. JT sat on the edge of the old sofa, boots still on, nursing a tin cup of coffee that had gone lukewarm hours ago. The rifles leaned in the corner, cleaned and oiled, the smell of Hoppe’s No. 9 mixing with woodsmoke and the faint copper tang that always seemed to cling to the place after a kill. Mike sprawled in the recliner, flipping through a yellowed outdoor magazine without reading it. Their father sat at the scarred oak table, methodically breaking down a sandwich into neat squares he never quite ate. The silence between them felt thicker than the air had that afternoon. JT cleared his throat. “That buck in the clearing…” His father’s hands stopped. Just for a second. Then he picked up another square of bread and meat, eyes fixed on the plate. “What about it?” “You almost… you had your hand out. Like you were gonna pet it.” Mike snorted without looking up. “Bullshit. Dad doesn’t pet game. He drops it.” “I saw it,” JT said, quieter. The words felt sticky in his mouth. “You both looked… gone for a second.” Their father set the sandwich down carefully, brush crumbs into a little pile with one thick finger. When he spoke, his voice was the same gravelly baritone JT had grown up hearing at ball games and over the roar of the outboard, but something under it had gone flat, like a radio station drifting off frequency. “You fell behind. Got turned around. Cold does that. Makes the eyes play tricks.” He stood, chair legs scraping. “We’re not talking about ghosts in the trees. We’re talking about meat in the freezer. Tomorrow we go farther east. Toward the old burn.” He walked to the window, looked out into the black, and stayed there. Mike finally glanced over, smirk fading into something closer to unease. “Kid’s been jumpy since we got here. Always was. Remember that time he swore the lake was whispering?” JT didn’t answer. He remembered. He’d been nine. The water had said his name in a voice like reeds rubbing together. Nobody else had heard it. He’d learned to shut up after that. Later, after Mike had snoring upstairs and their father had banked the fire and gone to his room without another word, JT sat alone by the dying light. He kept replaying the clearing: the buck’s chest rising and falling too slow, too deep; the way his father’s lips had parted like he was about to speak a name he shouldn’t know. The animal hadn’t smelled of musk or fear. It had smelled clean, almost sweet, like wet leaves after first frost. And when it vanished, it hadn’t crashed through brush. It had simply… stepped sideways out of the world. He rubbed his eyes hard enough to see sparks. Maybe he was losing it. Cabin fever in reverse—too much open country pressing in. Sleep came in thin, bad scraps. In one dream the lodge walls breathed. In another, caribou stood in perfect rows outside the windows, all of them watching with those same flat, patient eyes, waiting for someone to open the door. Morning arrived gray and windless. Frost silvered the porch rail. Their father was already outside, checking the truck’s tires, face set in that same closed expression. He barely looked at JT when the boy came down the steps. “Eat something,” was all he said. “Long day.” They drove east along the logging road that petered out into two ruts and then nothing. The spruce closed in overhead until the sky became a broken strip of pewter. JT rode in the back again, rifles between his knees, watching the trees slide past. Every so often he thought he saw movement—something pale keeping pace just beyond the trunks—but when he blinked it was only shadow and branch. They parked at the edge of the old burn, a scar of blackened snags and new growth that looked like the land had tried and failed to heal. The air here felt even heavier, charged, as if a storm were building under the soil. “Spread out,” their father said. “Fifty yards. Call if you jump something.” His voice was all business, the way it always had been. But he never met JT’s eyes. JT moved left, boots crunching through a crust of old snow and ash. The silence was absolute—no birds, no squirrel chatter, not even the usual distant drone of a plane. Just his own breathing and the soft tick of cooling metal somewhere behind him. He kept glancing back toward Mike’s orange vest, a bright smear between the trunks. Their father was already out of sight. An hour crawled by. Then two. JT’s legs ached from the cold. He stopped beside a half-fallen tamarack and tried to shake the feeling that the woods were rearranging themselves when he wasn’t looking. The trails seemed wrong—too smooth, as if something large had been pacing the same circuits over and over, polishing the ground. A twig snapped to his right. He turned, rifle half-raised, heart slamming. Nothing. Just more black trunks and the pale ghosts of birch. Then, farther in, a shape. Tall. Antlered. Standing perfectly still in a small glade where the light fell strangely flat, as if filtered through dirty glass. The caribou was bigger than the one yesterday. Its coat was the color of old bone. And its eyes—those same dark, calm eyes—were fixed not on him but on something just past his shoulder. JT didn’t turn. He knew, without knowing how, that if he looked behind him he would see his father standing there again, hand outstretched, mouth already forming words that didn’t belong in a human throat. The animal took one slow step forward. Then another. It wasn’t afraid. It looked… expectant. JT’s finger found the trigger guard. The metal felt greasy, cold. He thought of the first caribou of the season, the way it had waited for the bullet like a man waiting for a late bus. He thought of his father’s trembling hand. Of the silence that had settled over the lodge like dust. “Dad?” he called, voice cracking. “Mike?” No answer. Only the soft, rhythmic sound of the caribou’s breathing, deep and unhurried, matching the pulse that thundered in JT’s ears. The buck lowered its head slightly. Not a threat. An invitation. And somewhere in the trees behind him, JT heard the dry scrape of a boot sole on frost. Slow. Deliberate. Coming closer. He still didn’t turn around. The woods waited with him, patient as the animal’s eyes.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong adherence to the Stephen King-inspired surreal horror style requested. The author successfully maintains JT's perspective as an unreliable narrator caught between reality and something more sinister. The writing captures the isolation and creeping dread effectively, with the lodge setting providing an appropriately claustrophobic backdrop despite the wilderness location. The characterization is nuanced, particularly in how JT's family members respond to his observations. The father's defensive deflection and Mike's dismissive attitude feel authentic to family dynamics where uncomfortable truths are avoided. JT himself is well-developed as a character who has learned to suppress his perceptions due to past dismissals, creating believable internal conflict. The dialogue serves multiple purposes effectively - revealing character relationships, building tension, and advancing the supernatural elements. The father's explanation about cold making "eyes play tricks" and his abrupt topic change demonstrate how the family avoids confronting the strange occurrences. Mike's casual cruelty in referencing JT's childhood experience with the whispering lake adds depth to their sibling dynamic. The prose maintains consistent quality throughout, with particularly strong sensory details like the "copper tang" in the lodge and the caribou smelling "clean, almost sweet, like wet leaves after first frost." The pacing builds tension methodically, from the uncomfortable lodge scene through the night of bad dreams to the final confrontation in the burned forest. The supernatural elements are handled with appropriate subtlety. The caribou's unnatural behavior, the family's strange reactions, and the final scene where JT hears footsteps behind him but refuses to turn create genuine unease without over-explaining the threat. The ending delivers on the promised tension buildup with JT trapped between the expectant animal and the approaching presence behind him. Some minor weaknesses include occasional redundancy in describing the caribou's eyes and behavior, and a few instances where the prose edges toward being slightly overwrought, though it generally maintains restraint appropriate to the genre. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 15 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 5 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17
