+ Scp: Notes from the Future — Score: 83.7
Prompt:
Write an SCP report about a researcher who begins finding handwritten notes from their future self, warning about a containment breach that hasn't happened yet. The notes become increasingly desperate and incoherent, sometimes containing information that contradicts known reality. Include the Foundation's attempts to determine if this is an actual temporal anomaly or psychological manipulation by a contained entity, and the researcher's struggle to trust their own perception. First person (researcher's perspective with interspersed notes), past tense. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
**Personal Account Log: Dr. Elias Voss, Site-19 Research Division** **Date of Compilation: ██/██/20██** **Clearance: Level 3 Restricted** I began finding the notes on a Tuesday morning, three weeks after Transfer Order 19-Delta assigned me to monitor SCP-████’s containment chamber. The first one rested on my desk keyboard when I unlocked my office. It was written in my own handwriting on standard Foundation letterhead, the paper slightly crumpled as if handled roughly. *Elias—* *The breach is coming. Not the one they briefed you on. Something older. SCP-████ will not hold past 03:17 on the 14th. I have already watched the secondary bulkheads fail. Trust none of the cameras after that hour. Burn this after reading. —You* I checked the security footage myself. No one entered my office between 0700 and 0812. The note’s ink, under basic UV, looked ordinary. I logged it as a prank and shredded it, though I kept a digital scan. Two days later a second note appeared inside my locked personal locker in the Level-3 break room. *They will tell you it is stress. It is not. I am writing this from the corridor outside Observation Booth B after the lights went red. Your clearance badge will not open Door 19-C anymore; the codes change when the entity rewrites the local timeline. Do not eat the cafeteria stew on the 12th. It is contaminated with residual thaumaturgy. —Future Elias* I submitted both scans to Site Security and to Temporal Anomalies Desk. Agent Marquez ran them through handwriting analysis: 98.7 % match to my current script, including the slight leftward slant I developed after the surgery last year. The Foundation’s first working theory was psychological manipulation by a contained cognitohazard or memetic vector, possibly SCP-████ itself projecting false memories or inducing confabulation. They ordered a full psych eval and a week of Class-A amnestics “as precaution.” I refused the amnestics; I wanted to keep the data. The third note arrived during a routine containment check. I found it tucked into the spine of my personal copy of the Special Containment Procedures binder. *The breach is not a breach. It is a rewrite. I watched you die twice already. Once by spike through the throat when the entity phase-shifted the floor. Once by your own sidearm after the notes started lying. I am trying a third path. Ink is wrong here—check the spectrum. They will never believe you. —Me* Ink analysis from the Site-19 materials lab returned the same afternoon. Trace elements in the pigment did not match any standard office pen, nor any known Foundation-issue marker, nor the permanent inks used in secure document production. The report listed anomalous concentrations of an unidentified lanthanide isotope and a carbon-14 signature inconsistent with contemporary paper stocks. Temporal Anomalies Desk upgraded the file to possible Class-T temporal anomaly. Psychological Desk countered that a sophisticated memetic agent could induce the subject (me) to manufacture the notes while under influence, complete with false chemical signatures if the agent affected perception of lab results. I began photographing every surface of my office and locker before leaving each night. The fourth note still appeared, this time written on the back of a printed incident report that had not yet been generated. *03:17 is wrong. It is 02:41. Or 04:09. The clocks lie after the first rewrite. I saw D-Class 4491 walk through solid steel. I saw you order the lockdown that killed twelve researchers. Do not trust the version of me that tells you to open the door. That one is compromised. This one might be too. The ink stings when I write. Reality is thinning. —Elias who remembers the fire* My handwriting was still mine, but the pressure marks grew erratic. Sentences fractured. I stopped sleeping more than three hours a night. Every time I closed my eyes I saw the secondary bulkheads buckling inward in a way that contradicted the structural blueprints I had memorized. Foundation command assigned two escorts and continuous biometric monitoring. They ran me through three consecutive Scranton Reality Anchors. The notes continued. Fifth note, found inside a sealed evidence bag that had been empty when I logged it: *Contradiction: SCP-████ was never contained. We invented the designation after the first rewrite to give the event a name. The entity is the notes. Or the notes are the entity wearing my hand. I no longer know which version of the cafeteria stew killed me. I no longer know if you are reading this before or after I put the gun in my mouth. Ink analysis will keep saying the same thing because the isotope is from a future that already failed. Trust nothing. Especially not the next note that claims to be from further ahead. —Fragment* I presented the full set to the joint Temporal/Psychological review board. Dr. Patel argued for immediate Class-B amnestic treatment and reassignment. Dr. Ruiz from Temporal insisted the isotope data constituted evidence of retrocausal information transfer and recommended isolating me inside a Faraday-anchored suite with continuous video. I sat between them and watched my own hands tremble while I read the notes aloud. My voice sounded like someone else’s. Sixth note appeared on the inside of my forearm when I woke in the medical bay after a panic attack. Security cameras showed no contact. The words were cut shallowly into the skin in the same script, ink seeping from the wounds that matched the earlier chemical profile. *They will decide it is psychological. They will decide it is temporal. Both decisions open the door. I have written this sentence seventeen times across seventeen failed containment attempts. Each time the ink is slightly wronger. Each time you believe a little less. The breach is the belief. Or the disbelief. I cannot tell which version of reality I am bleeding into now. Do not look for the next note. It will find you after the lights go red. —Last* I no longer trusted the date on the wall calendar. I no longer trusted the face in the mirror that still looked like mine. Foundation technicians installed additional anchors and began preparing a full cognitive quarantine protocol. I dictated this account into a recorder while two Level-4 personnel watched. My voice kept slipping into the cadence of the later notes. The final note—if it is final—was waiting inside the recorder’s battery compartment when I opened it to change the cells. *Elias. You are reading this after the board meeting. The breach already happened. Or it is still three days away. Or it never will because this sentence collapses the waveform. I am writing with a pen that does not exist yet. The isotope is the only constant. Everything else, including your name, is provisional. Trust the ink or trust the fear. Both will kill you. I am so tired of dying. —The one who keeps writing* I have locked the recorder inside a Type-IV secure container. Ink analysis on the skin note and the final scrap returned identical anomalous spectra. Temporal Desk has requested transfer of all materials to Site-17 for deeper chronometric study. Psychological Desk has scheduled me for mandatory evaluation at 0800 tomorrow. I do not know which version of me will walk into that room. I do not know if the bulkheads will hold. I do not know if I already wrote this log after the lights went red. End of account. Word count: 998
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This SCP report demonstrates strong adherence to the Foundation's clinical documentation style while effectively building psychological horror through temporal uncertainty. The author successfully captures the bureaucratic tone characteristic of SCP writing, complete with redacted information, classification levels, and interdepartmental conflicts between Temporal and Psychological desks. The narrative structure is well-executed, using the deteriorating notes as both plot device and character development tool. The progression from coherent warnings to fragmented, contradictory messages effectively mirrors the protagonist's declining mental state and growing uncertainty about reality. The handwriting analysis, isotope testing, and other scientific elements feel authentic to the SCP universe while serving the story's themes. The characterization of Dr. Voss is believable and nuanced. His responses feel genuine - the initial skepticism, methodical documentation, growing paranoia, and ultimate breakdown follow a logical psychological trajectory. The supporting characters (Agent Marquez, Dr. Patel, Dr. Ruiz) serve their roles effectively without feeling like mere exposition vehicles. The prose maintains clinical precision while building dread through accumulating details. Phrases like "Reality is thinning" and "I am so tired of dying" provide emotional weight without breaking the documentary tone. The imagery is restrained but effective - the bulkheads buckling, lights going red, and ink seeping from wounds create visceral moments within the analytical framework. However, the piece occasionally relies on telling rather than showing, particularly in describing Voss's emotional state. Some transitions between notes feel slightly mechanical, and while the ambiguity serves the theme, certain plot elements could benefit from more subtle development. The ending, while appropriately uncertain, borders on being overwrought with its repetitive structure. The temporal paradox concept is handled creatively, avoiding simple time travel clichés by making the notes themselves potentially the anomaly. The escalating contradiction and self-reference create genuine uncertainty about what's real, which is the story's greatest strength. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 16 Coherent: 17 Meandering: 4 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 6 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 3 Overwrought: 6 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 19.0, Believable Character Actions: 17.0, Nuanced Characters: 16.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16.0, Elegant Prose: 17.0, Emotionally Engaging: 17.0, Emotionally Complex: 16.0, Coherent: 17.0, Meandering: 4.0, Weak Dialogue: 3.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 6.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4.0, Amateurish: 3.0, Purple Prose: 3.0, Overwrought: 6.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1.0, Unearned Transformations: 2.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 16.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 17.0, Overall Impression: 17.0
+ Scp: Unmarked Doors Archive — Score: 76.3
Prompt:
Write an SCP entry about a series of identical unmarked doors appearing in abandoned buildings worldwide that lead to a massive, architecturally impossible archive. Documents within reference a previous global civilization destroyed by an unspecified containment failure. Foundation researchers discover references to their own organization under a different name, including personnel files for current staff dated centuries ago. Include exploration logs revealing sections of the archive actively being removed by entities resembling hazmat teams who flee when approached, and evidence that O5 Council members have visited the archive without authorization. Third person (exploration team perspective) with recovered document excerpts. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
**Item #:** SCP-8742 **Object Class:** Euclid **Special Containment Procedures:** All instances of SCP-8742-1 are to be secured under Standard Containment Protocol 400-Delta upon discovery. Mobile Task Force Epsilon-11 (“Nine-Tailed Fox”) elements are to establish perimeter exclusion zones of no less than 500 meters around affected structures. Civilian access is prohibited under cover of structural condemnation and hazardous material remediation. Exploration of SCP-8742-2 is restricted to personnel with Level 4 clearance or higher, equipped with standard anomalous environment kits including remote telemetry beacons and cognitive resistance agents. Any observation of entities designated SCP-8742-3 is to be reported immediately; pursuit is forbidden. O5 Command is to be notified of any recovered documents referencing Foundation personnel or the O5 Council. Unauthorized Foundation personnel discovered within SCP-8742-2 are subject to immediate amnesticization and disciplinary review. **Description:** SCP-8742 refers to a series of identical unmarked doors (SCP-8742-1) that spontaneously manifest in abandoned buildings across the globe. Each SCP-8742-1 is a featureless gray metal door set into a plain frame, lacking handles, hinges, or identifying marks. Instances frequently exhibit weathering, corrosion, and material degradation inconsistent with the age of the host structure; several doors have been dated via radioisotope and dendrochronological proxies to approximately 200–400 years older than the buildings containing them. Opening any SCP-8742-1 grants access to a contiguous extradimensional space designated SCP-8742-2: a vast, architecturally impossible archive complex of indeterminate size. Internal geometry violates Euclidean principles; corridors loop, floors transition without transition points, and ceiling heights fluctuate between 3 and 40 meters without structural supports. The archive contains an estimated several billion physical documents—paper, vellum, clay tablets, and unknown composite media—organized on shelves, in cabinets, and in free-standing stacks that extend beyond visual range. Climate control is autonomous and non-anomalous in appearance; ambient temperature and humidity remain constant at 18 °C and 45 % relative humidity. Documents recovered from SCP-8742-2 consistently describe a prior global civilization that collapsed following an unspecified “containment failure.” Linguistic analysis indicates a hybrid of proto-Indo-European roots and unknown script systems. Multiple recovered items directly reference the SCP Foundation under variant nomenclature (“Secure Continuum Protocol,” “Foundation for the Preservation of Continuity,” and “Overseer Directive Authority”). Personnel files matching current Foundation staff, including full biographical data, psychological profiles, and anomalous exposure records, appear with dates ranging from 300 to 1,200 years prior to the present. O5 Council designations (O5-1 through O5-13) appear repeatedly. **Addendum 8742-A: Exploration Log Excerpts – MTF Epsilon-11 Team “Archive-3”** *Team Lead: Agent R. Vasquez. Team composition: Vasquez, Specialist Kline, Researcher H. Okonkwo, Security Officer M. Tran. Entry via SCP-8742-1 instance located in decommissioned textile mill, Lyon, France. Date: 20██-03-14.* **00:12 – Vasquez:** Door opened without resistance. Immediate transition into a main gallery. Shelves rise out of sight. No sound except our breathing and the soft hum of whatever keeps the air stable. Floor is polished stone that doesn’t scuff. Kline, mark the entry with a beacon. **00:47 – Okonkwo:** First document pull. Bound ledger, title translates roughly as “Incident Log: Continuity Breach Event Zero.” Describes a planetary-scale failure of containment protocols leading to “total informational cascade and civilizational reset.” No specific agent named. Multiple references to “the Overseers who remained behind.” **01:19 – Tran:** Movement, far end of aisle twelve. Visual confirmation: four bipedal figures in full hazmat-style suits, yellow-orange, no insignia. They’re pulling entire shelf sections free and loading them onto floating platforms that leave no tracks. When I called out they stopped, turned, and fled down a side corridor that wasn’t there a second earlier. Did not pursue per protocol. **01:41 – Vasquez:** We advanced carefully. Found residual scorch marks and a discarded glove. Material is non-standard polymer; analysis pending. More documents on the floor—scattered like they dropped them in haste. *Recovered Document Excerpt 8742-R7 (partial translation):* “…Personnel File: Okonkwo, H. – Continuity Specialist, Cycle 47. Exposure history: Keter-class memetic agents, three successful containments. Date of last active service: [illegible, estimated ████ years pre-present]. Status: Recycled into successor Foundation framework. Note: Subject retains latent recall signatures. Recommend monitoring for spontaneous archive access.” **02:08 – Kline:** Another file. My name. Same face in the attached photograph, same scar on the left hand. Dated three hundred years ago. Psychological evaluation notes “recurring dreams of unmarked doors.” This isn’t possible. **02:33 – Okonkwo:** Gallery opens into a circular atrium. Central pedestal holds a bound volume titled “Overseer Visitations – Unauthorized.” Entries list times, entry points, and signatures. Multiple O5 designations appear. Most recent: O5-7, timestamp equivalent to six months ago, entry via an instance we have not yet secured. Handwriting analysis matches known samples of current O5-7. **03:01 – Vasquez:** We’re being watched. Shadows moving at the edges of vision—more hazmat entities, at least a dozen. They’re systematically removing sections. Entire corridors vanish when they finish. We’re falling back to the entry before the path closes. **03:22 – Tran:** Final visual: one entity paused long enough to look directly at the camera. Visor opaque. It raised one gloved hand in what might have been a warning or a salute, then the entire far wing of the archive simply… wasn’t there anymore. Empty space, then a new wall of shelves. *End of primary exploration log. Team extracted successfully. No injuries. Cognitive battery results within acceptable parameters. All recovered documents transferred to Site-19 Secure Archive under Level 5 lockdown.* **Addendum 8742-B: Additional Recovered Excerpts** *Document 8742-R12 (fragment, vellum, estimated age 800+ years):* “The Archive remembers every cycle. When containment fails, the doors open and the next Foundation is born from the records we leave. The hazmat teams are not ours. They clean what must not be known by the next iteration. The Overseers have always known. Some of them walk the stacks even now, reading their own future files. Do not follow them.” *Document 8742-R19 (personnel dossier, paper stock matching modern Foundation letterhead but aged inconsistently):* “Subject: Current O5 Council. Status: Recurring. Warning: Members have made unsanctioned entries into the Continuity Archive on seventeen recorded occasions this cycle. Motive unknown. Possibility of deliberate information seeding or personal continuity preservation. Recommend immediate review by surviving Overseers of prior cycle. If none remain, the Archive will enforce its own silence.” **Addendum 8742-C: Incident Report Summary** On 20██-04-02, remote telemetry from a newly manifested SCP-8742-1 in rural Manitoba detected an unidentified individual matching the biometric profile of O5-4 entering the archive without escort or logged authorization. Subject remained inside for 47 minutes before exiting. When confronted by on-site MTF elements, O5-4 produced valid override credentials and ordered all recordings sealed under O5-eyes-only protocol. The individual declined comment. Subsequent audit of O5 Council movements revealed three additional unlogged absences correlating with SCP-8742-1 manifestation events. Exploration of SCP-8742-2 continues under heightened restrictions. The archive is visibly shrinking in mapped sectors as SCP-8742-3 entities remove material. Estimated remaining volume of the accessible archive has decreased by approximately 11 % since first discovery. Research priority remains determination of the nature of the prior containment failure and the precise relationship between the current Foundation and the civilization that preceded it. *Final note from Exploration Team Lead Vasquez (debrief transcript):* “The doors are older than the buildings. The files are older than us. And someone already wearing our faces has been walking those halls for longer than we have existed. Whatever failed last time… the people in the hazmat suits are still cleaning it up. And our Overseers already know the way in.”
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This SCP entry demonstrates strong technical competence in mimicking the Foundation's clinical documentation style while weaving a compelling narrative about cyclical civilizations and temporal mysteries. The piece successfully fulfills most prompt requirements, incorporating unmarked doors, an impossible archive, references to previous civilizations, personnel files dated centuries ago, hazmat entities removing documents, and unauthorized O5 visits. The writing maintains the characteristic SCP tone throughout - dry, clinical, and bureaucratic while building genuine tension and mystery. The exploration logs feel authentic to the format, with realistic radio chatter and procedural language. The gradual revelation of information creates effective dramatic pacing, from the initial discovery of anachronistic personnel files to the disturbing implications of cyclical Foundation iterations. Particularly effective is the layering of mysteries: the hazmat entities systematically removing evidence, the O5 Council's unauthorized visits, and the suggestion that current Foundation members are somehow recycled from previous iterations. The recovered document excerpts serve as excellent worldbuilding elements that deepen the horror through implication rather than explicit description. However, the piece has some weaknesses. The characters in the exploration logs, while functional, lack distinct personalities beyond their roles. Agent Vasquez, Specialist Kline, and others speak in similar voices with minimal individual characterization. The dialogue serves its informational purpose but doesn't reveal much about the speakers as people. The concept, while well-executed, treads familiar SCP territory regarding cyclical apocalypses and Foundation conspiracies. The "previous iteration of our organization" trope has been explored before, though this version adds some fresh elements with the active document removal and O5 involvement. The prose occasionally becomes slightly overwrought in its attempts to build atmosphere, particularly in descriptions of the archive's impossible geometry. Some phrases like "informational cascade and civilizational reset" feel unnecessarily complex for the clinical style being emulated. The ending maintains appropriate ambiguity for the SCP format while providing enough resolution to feel complete. The final quote from Vasquez effectively summarizes the existential dread without over-explaining the mystery. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 12 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 14 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 4 Weak Dialogue: 8 Tell-Don't-Show: 6 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 5 Purple Prose: 7 Overwrought: 8 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 16

+ Classic sci-fi: Offworlder (Le Guin) — Score: 81.8
Prompt:
Classic sci-fi: Offworlder (Author style: Le Guin) Write a chapter following a young diplomatic envoy sent to address tensions on a distant human colony where humanity has diverged into distinct subspecies through both natural adaptation and separation of lineages. Against protocol, the envoy slips away from official functions to walk the streets alone, encountering markets, ordinary citizens, and social structures that contradict the official briefings. Upon returning to their quarters, the envoy is reprimanded by Command, and is tasked with approaching the stationed Minister of Science (a political posting rather than scientific). A challenging conversation ensues, in which the envoy's wits and will are tested, by a seasoned political operator looking to test the mettle of the offworlder. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
**Chapter III: The Streets of Veyra** The air of Veyra carried the mineral tang of its red-dust seas, thin and sharp under the double suns. Envoy Lira Voss had been three days on the surface before she broke protocol. The official briefings—sealed crystals from Command, polished by analysts who had never set foot here—spoke of a stable human colony, subspecies marked only by adaptive traits: the long-limbed *Aetheri* of the high plateaus, the broad-chested *Terranex* of the denser lowlands, lineages separated for centuries yet still one people under the Compact. Social cohesion high. Hierarchy rational. Markets free but monitored. All of it neat as a star-chart. Against that neatness, Lira left the compound after the third formal reception. She folded her envoy’s cloak around her—deep indigo, lined against the cold of orbital transit, its hood a practical shadow—and slipped the perimeter gate while the guards changed. No one stopped her. Offworlders were rare enough that curiosity outweighed suspicion, or so she hoped. The streets of the capital unrolled in stepped terraces of pale stone and living wood. Locals moved with an economy of gesture that made her own walk feel clumsy. They dressed minimally: the Aetheri in light wraps that left arms and collarbones bare to the dry wind, the Terranex in short kilts and open vests that showed the dense musculature of their denser gravity-adapted frames. Skin tones ranging from copper to ash, eyes large or hooded according to lineage. Her cloak drew stares like a foreign moon. Children pointed. A market vendor paused mid-haggle, eyes tracking the heavy fabric as if it were armor or mourning. Lira kept her pace even, hood low, and let the city speak. The market contradicted everything. Briefings had described regulated exchanges under Compact tariffs. What she found was a living braid of barter and gift. An Aetheri elder traded dried high-plateau fungi for a Terranex youth’s carved bone tools—not coin, not credit, but a story of shared drought three generations back. Social structures bent around kinship webs that crossed subspecies lines: a Terranex mother nursing an Aetheri infant while arguing good-naturedly with a distant cousin over water rights. Hierarchies existed, yes, but they were fluid, earned in council circles rather than inherited titles. Where the briefings spoke of “potential friction points,” Lira saw laughter, arguments that ended in shared meals, and a quiet pride that did not need the Compact’s approval. Humanity had diverged—bones lengthened, lungs deepened, skins thickened against radiation—and yet the divergence had not fractured them. It had woven them tighter, like the dual suns casting double shadows that always overlapped. She walked until the second sun dipped, tasting roasted seed-cakes offered freely by a vendor who only asked where her cloak came from. “Offworld,” Lira said. The woman nodded as if that explained the coldness of the cloth. Ordinary citizens spoke of the Minister of Science with wry affection or mild contempt—“a political posting, yes, but he keeps the stars from falling on us”—never with the dread the briefings implied. Tension existed, but it was the tension of growth, not fracture. Lira returned to the compound with dust on her boots and a mind full of unrecorded truths. Command was waiting in her quarters, voice cold through the sealed crystal. “You left the perimeter. Against protocol. The colony is not stable enough for solo reconnaissance.” Lira stood straight. “The briefings are incomplete. Social structures are adaptive, not hierarchical as stated. Markets function on reciprocity webs that cross subspecies. The divergences—natural and lineage—have produced resilience, not the isolation models predicted.” “Your observations are noted and irrelevant. You will approach the stationed Minister of Science tomorrow. He is a political operator first. Test him. Do not let him test you. Report any leverage. End.” The crystal went dark. Lira removed the cloak, felt the lighter air on her skin, and wondered how much of Command’s caution was genuine and how much was the old fear of difference. The Minister received her in a high chamber open to the wind, its walls of translucent stone that filtered the double light into soft amber. He was Terranex by build—broad, deliberate—with the silvered hair of long service and eyes that measured without haste. No scientific instruments cluttered the room; only low couches, a basin of clear water, and a single hanging map of the colony’s dual continents. He rose with the grace of someone who had never needed to hurry to prove importance. “Envoy Voss. The cloak again. Curious choice for Veyra’s heat.” “It keeps the offworld cold in,” she said, refusing the seat until he gestured. “And the stares tell me more than briefings.” He smiled, thin and practiced. “Ah. You walked the markets. Against protocol, I assume. Command does love its protocols. Tell me what you saw that their crystals missed.” Lira met his gaze. “Reciprocity where they predicted competition. Kinship across lineages where they mapped separation. Subspecies traits worn as tools, not barriers. The Aetheri climb higher; the Terranex dig deeper. Both feed the same table. Your people are not fracturing. They are specializing.” “Specializing.” He poured water for them both, the sound deliberate. “A generous word. Command prefers ‘diverging beyond Compact norms.’ They sent a young envoy—untested, idealistic—to see if the norms still hold. Or to justify tightening them. Which are you here for, Offworlder?” “To address tensions,” she said. “Not invent them.” “Tensions are currency.” He leaned forward, voice soft as the wind through stone. “I am Minister of Science by title, politician by necessity. The Compact funds our atmospheric stabilizers, our gene-line monitors. In return they want reports of stability that match their models. You saw the markets. You saw the truth. Will you report the markets as they are—living, cross-lineage, inconvenient—or will you shape them into the friction points Command expects? Your wits are sharp. Your will is unproven. I have seen envoys break on less.” Lira drank, tasting mineral and patience. “If I shape the truth to their models, the colony loses the adaptations that keep it alive. If I report as I saw, Command may tighten the leash. Either way, you test me for leverage. What do you want, Minister? Reassurance that the young offworlder can be managed, or an ally who will carry the actual streets back to the stars?” He laughed once, genuine and short. “Both, if possible. Mettle is rare. Most arrive with their cloaks and their certainties and leave with neither. You walked alone. You listened. That is a beginning. The conversation is not over. Return tomorrow without the cloak. Dress as the streets dress. See how the stares change when you stop announcing yourself as other. Then we will speak of tensions that matter—and of how an envoy might serve both Compact and colony without becoming either’s tool.” Lira rose. The double light caught the edges of his silver hair like twin warnings. “I will return. And I will decide what I carry back.” Outside, the second sun set fully. The cloak felt heavier than before. On the terraces below, Aetheri and Terranex moved together in the cooling dusk, their minimal dress a language of belonging she had only begun to read. Command demanded reports. The streets demanded honesty. Between them stood the Minister of Science, political to the bone, waiting to see which way the young envoy’s will would bend. Lira walked back under the first stars of Veyra’s night, already drafting the words she would not yet send.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates a strong grasp of Le Guin's style and successfully creates a compelling science fiction narrative that addresses the prompt's requirements. The writing exhibits Le Guin's characteristic restraint, philosophical depth, and focus on anthropological observation rather than action-driven plot. The worldbuilding is particularly effective, presenting the Aetheri and Terranex subspecies with clear physical and cultural distinctions while avoiding heavy-handed exposition. The author skillfully contrasts the sterile official briefings with the lived reality Lira discovers, creating tension between institutional knowledge and experiential truth that echoes Le Guin's frequent themes about the limitations of external observation. The prose maintains an appropriate tone throughout - measured, thoughtful, and precise. Descriptions like "the air of Veyra carried the mineral tang of its red-dust seas" and "briefings had described regulated exchanges under Compact tariffs. What she found was a living braid of barter and gift" demonstrate strong imagery without becoming overwrought. The writing shows restraint in avoiding excessive metaphor or flowery language. Character development is nuanced, particularly in the portrayal of Lira as someone caught between duty and discovery, and the Minister as a complex political figure who is neither antagonist nor ally but something more interesting - a pragmatic operator testing her mettle. The dialogue feels natural and serves multiple purposes: revealing character, advancing plot, and exploring themes. The structure follows the prompt faithfully: the unauthorized walk, the reprimand, and the challenging conversation with the Minister. The pacing allows for both observation and reflection, building to the political confrontation that tests Lira's resolve. Some minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the prose becomes slightly dense with concepts, and the ending, while thematically appropriate, could have pushed the conflict further. The piece also relies somewhat heavily on the familiar trope of the naive outsider discovering hidden truths, though it executes this competently. The piece succeeds in creating a complete narrative arc within its constraints while establishing a larger world and conflict that extends beyond the chapter's boundaries. It demonstrates sophisticated understanding of both the source material's style and the science fiction genre's capacity for social commentary. + Biopunk: Polymerase Chain Reaction — Score: 80.9
Prompt:
Biopunk -- Polymerase Chain Reaction Write a chapter set in a near-future post-apocalyptic world where corporate genetic engineering was designed with built-in obsolescence—engineered DNA lasting only one generation to maximize profits before societal collapse. Follow a biohacker working with salvaged equipment to splice viable food crops for their settlement using rapidly degrading genetic material. As world building / window dressing, include details about jury-rigged lab equipment, and the physical toll (and unexpected upsides) of using untested genetic modifications as medicine. The plot of this chapter is centred around a black market contract. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
**Biopunk — Polymerase Chain Reaction** The settlement’s grow-domes clung to the cracked highway like scabs on old asphalt. Inside Lab Three—once a roadside clinic, now a riot of scavenged polycarbonate and duct-taped blowers—Mira hunched over the thermal cycler she’d rebuilt from a broken incubator and scavenged Peltier chips. The machine’s fan screamed like a dying turbine. Empty canisters from HelixCorp’s “PermaYield” line, their corporate logos half-scoured by acid rain, now held her reagents: Taq polymerase she’d pulled from freeze-dried stock that still carried the company’s obsolescence watermark, dNTPs that smelled faintly of solvent, and primers she’d synthesized on a jury-rigged phosphoramidite rig powered by a hand-crank generator. HelixCorp and the rest of the biotech giants had engineered everything—wheat, soy, even the therapeutic vectors people injected for radiation damage—with molecular kill-switches. DNA that worked for one generation, then scrambled its own reading frames or inserted lethal nonsense codons. Profits maximized, then the Collapse hit and the patents became tombstones. Now the settlement’s seed stocks were failing. Last season’s maize had produced sterile cobs. This season’s seedlings yellowed after three weeks. Mira’s job was to splice something that would last longer than a single harvest cycle before the built-in rot took it. She worked under a black-market contract. The broker, a scarred woman who called herself only “Vex,” had delivered a sealed cryovial two nights ago along with half a crate of scavenged nucleotides and a promise of protein powder and antibiotics sufficient for twenty people through winter. The payload: a fragment of ancestral Triticum DNA recovered from a pre-Collapse seed vault, plus a proprietary promoter cassette that was supposed to silence the obsolescence sequences. Payment on delivery of fifty viable, multi-generation embryos. Failure meant the contract’s secondary clause—Vex’s enforcers would take Mira’s remaining lab gear and the settlement’s last functional centrifuge. Mira’s hands shook as she loaded the reaction tubes. The tremor was new. Three months earlier she had self-administered an untested CRISPR cocktail harvested from a dead courier’s med-kit to reverse the progressive neuropathy that left her fingers numb. The cocktail had been meant for corporate security personnel—modifications for enhanced peripheral nerve regeneration, plus an experimental mycotoxin resistance cassette. It worked, mostly. Sensation returned in waves of electric fire. The upside arrived later: she could now smell nucleotide degradation byproducts the way a sommelier smells cork taint, and her night vision had sharpened enough that she no longer needed the failing LED strips for fine pipetting. The toll was constant low-grade fever, periodic muscle spasms that made her drop tubes, and the knowledge that the same kill-switches lived in her own cells. One more generation of mitosis and the edits might unravel. She sealed the tubes and started the PCR. The machine’s display—an old tablet screen wired with alligator clips—showed the temperature curve climbing. Denature. Anneal. Extend. Forty cycles. Outside, the settlement’s wind turbines groaned. Someone hammered on the dome’s outer hatch. Mira ignored it. The black-market clock was ticking; Vex expected results by dawn. While the cycler ran she prepared the ligation. Empty HelixCorp canisters sat in a rack like metal skulls, their interiors lined with teflon tape to hold ethidium-free agarose gels and the restriction digests. She used a salvaged UV transilluminator whose bulbs she had rewound with copper wire from a solar inverter. The light flickered blue-white across the workbench, illuminating scars on her forearms where earlier mods had blistered during expression. One of those mods—an untested hemoglobin variant—let her work longer in the low-oxygen grow-domes without tiring. Another, a crude myostatin inhibitor, had given her the strength to haul water barrels but left her joints aching like old machinery. The products came off the cycler. She ran the gel. Bands appeared exactly where they should. Clean amplicons. She excised them with a razor blade sterilized over an alcohol flame, melted the agarose in a water bath heated by a salvaged immersion heater, and purified the DNA through a column packed with silica scraped from old hard drives. Ligation into a binary vector stripped of its original corporate barcodes. Transformation into competent cells she’d made competent with calcium chloride boiled from scavenged road salt and glycerol siphoned from a dead truck’s radiator. While the plates incubated in a yogurt-maker turned incubator, Mira checked the seed stock. The parental lines were already showing signs of the kill-switch: leaf tips curling, chloroplasts bleaching under the grow-lights. She would have one shot at electroporating the new constructs into callus tissue before the maternal genome finished degrading. The electroporator was three car batteries in series, a homemade capacitor bank, and electrodes cut from stainless steel spoons. She had calibrated the pulse by trial and error on earlier, less valuable cultures—most of which had died. A second knock, harder. Vex’s courier, probably. Mira wiped sweat from her eyes. The fever spiked. For a moment the lab lights flared too bright and she tasted copper. Then the enhanced olfaction kicked in again: she smelled the faint sweet-rot of the old HelixCorp primers starting to degrade even in the cold canisters. She sealed the remaining stock tighter. At 0400 the colonies appeared. White, healthy. She picked them, miniprepped with a chloroform substitute distilled from plastic waste, and sequenced the critical junctions on a nanopore device whose flow cell she had revived with detergent and prayer. The insert was clean. The obsolescence silencers sat in-frame. She moved immediately to the plant tissue. By first light she had twenty-three calli that took the construct and began green differentiation. Not fifty. Close enough that she could argue the remainder would follow once she optimized the recovery medium. She packaged fifteen of the strongest in sterile tubes lined with moist filter paper, sealed them inside an emptied PermaYield canister, and stepped outside. Vex waited under the turbine shadow, two armed figures behind her. Mira handed over the canister. “Multi-gen potential confirmed by junction sequencing. Silencer cassette active. You’ll get the rest after the first greenhouse trial—unless you want sterile dirt instead of grain.” Vex weighed the canister, cracked the lid just enough to see the green tissue, then nodded once. A crate of sealed protein packs and antibiotic vials was unstrapped from a cargo bike. No words about the shortfall. Contracts in the ruins were enforced by results more than fine print. Mira carried the supplies back inside. Her hands still trembled, but the crops would root. For one more generation, maybe two, the settlement would eat. After that the DNA would try to die again, and she would be back at the cycler with whatever reagents she could scavenge next. The kill-switches never truly slept. Neither did she. (Word count: 998)
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This biopunk chapter demonstrates strong technical writing and worldbuilding within the specified parameters. The author successfully creates a convincing post-apocalyptic setting where corporate genetic engineering with built-in obsolescence has led to societal collapse, and follows biohacker Mira as she works to create viable crops using degrading genetic material. The technical aspects are particularly well-executed. The scientific terminology feels authentic without becoming incomprehensible, and the jury-rigged laboratory equipment is described with convincing detail - from the thermal cycler rebuilt with Peltier chips to the electroporator made from car batteries. The author clearly understands molecular biology and presents the PCR process and genetic modification procedures believably. The worldbuilding is efficient and immersive. Details like HelixCorp's "PermaYield" products with obsolescence watermarks, the corporate kill-switches in DNA, and the repurposed equipment create a vivid sense of a world built on the ruins of biotech capitalism. The physical descriptions of the settlement's grow-domes "clinging to cracked highway like scabs" establish the post-apocalyptic atmosphere effectively. Mira emerges as a compelling protagonist whose self-experimentation with untested genetic modifications adds both practical advantages and physical costs. Her enhanced senses and strength come with fever, tremors, and the knowledge that her own modifications may be temporary. This creates genuine stakes and character depth. The black market contract provides solid narrative structure, with clear objectives and consequences. Vex as the broker feels appropriately threatening without being cartoonish, and the transaction at the end maintains tension even when Mira delivers fewer samples than promised. However, the piece has some limitations. While Mira is well-developed, other characters remain fairly thin. The emotional engagement, while present, could be deeper - we understand Mira's situation intellectually but don't feel her desperation as viscerally as we might. The prose, while competent, occasionally relies on technical exposition where more sensory or emotional detail might serve better. The ending strikes an appropriate balance - neither falsely optimistic nor gratuitously dark. Mira succeeds partially, buying time for the settlement while acknowledging the ongoing struggle ahead. This feels earned given the established constraints of the world. The writing maintains consistent voice throughout, with sentences that flow naturally and technical passages integrated smoothly into the narrative. The imagery is generally strong, particularly in describing the makeshift laboratory and the physical effects of genetic modifications. + Iteration 3 — Avg Score: 81.0
+ Classic sci-fi: The Azra Gambit — Score: 70.7
Prompt:
Classic sci-fi (Author style: Asimov) The Azra Gambit Colonial mars is being mined by corporations who take leases on indentured labourers. The thing they are mining is Azra, a recently discovered exotic metal which accelerates radioactive decay to such a rate that it is greatly sought after for interstellar drives and weapons alike. This has created both a gold rush and an arms race as various interests vie for control and endeavour to unlock Azra's secrets. The story follows Arthur Neegan, a first generation settler and mining engineer. Upon discovering that his unassuming plot sits atop an immense Azra vein, he is subjected to a flurry of interest and scrutiny. Write the next chapter in this story, in which an armed retinue descends on Arthur's home and politely but forcefully invites him to a meeting with some unknown party off-world. The insignia look like that of the Antares diplomatic corp -- diplomatic in name only. Arthur finds himself in the centre of a political tug of war. The chapter involves a meeting with this unknown party, who makes Arthur an offer. The scene should be primarily dialogue, interspersed with vivid description & scene setting. It should sow hints of the larger intrigue, stakes & dangers. Include Asimov's trademark big-and-small-picture world building and retrofuturistic classic scifi vibe. The chapter begins with Arthur aboard the transfer vessel, wondering just what he's gotten involved in. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
The transfer vessel's air filtration system hums with a persistent mechanical drone, a low thrum that vibrates through the thin bulkheads and into Arthur Neegan's bones. He sits alone in the padded acceleration couch of the passenger module, the restraint harness still loose across his chest, and stares at the viewport where Mars dwindles to a rusty crescent. Red dust, domes, the distant glitter of orbital elevators—all of it shrinks against the black. He wonders, not for the first time, just what he has gotten himself involved in. Three days earlier the retinue had arrived without warning. Six figures in matte-gray environment suits, sidearms holstered but visible, their shoulder patches bearing the stylized star-and-circle of the Antares Diplomatic Corps. Diplomatic in name only, everyone on the colonies knew. They had been polite. They had been firm. "Mr. Neegan," the leader had said, voice filtered through the suit speaker, "your presence is requested off-world. A matter of mutual interest regarding the Azra deposit beneath Claim 47-B." Arthur had looked from the armed men to the modest pressure dome he had built with his own hands, to the assay samples still open on his workbench, and understood that refusal was not among the available options. Now the vessel—a compact transfer tug of the type that shuttles between the outer colonies and the Lagrange stations—accelerates on a quiet ion flare. The pilot, a silent woman with the same Antares insignia, has not spoken since departure. Arthur flexes his fingers. The metal is still under his nails: ordinary Martian basalt laced with the strange silvery sheen of Azra. A recently catalogued exotic, it catalyzes radioactive decay rates by factors of thousands. What had once required planetary-scale reactors could now power a starship drive the size of a cargo crate—or a warhead that could crack a moon. The corporations had descended like locusts. Indenture contracts proliferated. Leases on human labor became more valuable than the land itself. And Arthur, first-generation settler, mining engineer with a modest plot and no political friends, had the misfortune of sitting atop what the surveys now whispered might be the richest vein yet found. The docking clamps engage with a solid thunk. A soft chime sounds. The pilot turns. "You will be met." The airlock cycles. Beyond lies not a crowded commercial hub but a private annex: white corridors lit by steady fluorescent panels, the retro-futuristic aesthetic of mid-century optimism preserved in alloy and polymer. No advertising holos, no throngs of freighter crews. Only two more gray-suited escorts who fall in step without a word. Arthur walks between them, boots ringing softly on the deck plates. Through a viewport he glimpses the curve of a larger station, its radiating arms marked with the flags of a dozen Earth polities and the corporate sigils of the great mining houses—Helix Dynamics, Orion Resource Combine, the Sino-Pacific Consortium. Beyond them, the dark of interplanetary space, and somewhere out there the first experimental Azra drives already pushing survey ships toward the nearer stars. An arms race and a gold rush, fused into one. They usher him into a conference chamber. The walls are bare save for a single large screen currently showing a real-time schematic of the Martian crust, Claim 47-B highlighted in pulsing amber. At the far end of a long table sits a man in a civilian suit of impeccable cut, though the fabric looks slightly off—synthesized from station stock rather than Earth silk. His hair is silver at the temples, his expression one of practiced neutrality. No nameplate. No rank. Only the same Antares patch on a discreet lapel pin. "Mr. Neegan," the man says, rising just enough to incline his head. "Please, sit. I am Counselor Voss. Thank you for coming." Arthur remains standing a moment longer. "I wasn't given much choice." Voss smiles thinly. "Choice is a luxury the frontier rarely affords. Sit. We have little time, and the stakes are larger than either of us." Arthur sits. The chair is comfortable, almost luxuriously so after the hard couch of the tug. Voss folds his hands. "Your claim," Voss begins, "has registered anomalous density readings consistent with a primary Azra lode of approximately fourteen million metric tons. That figure is, of course, preliminary. But if confirmed, it would dwarf every other known deposit combined. The corporations already scent it. Helix has filed three overlapping lease challenges. Orion has begun buying up neighboring indentures. The Sino-Pacific Consortium has... other methods. And the various Earth governments—those still capable of projecting power this far—have taken notice. Azra is not merely wealth. It is the key to the next phase of human expansion. Whoever controls the high-grade veins controls the drives. Whoever controls the drives controls the stars. And the weapons." Arthur keeps his voice level. "I filed the claim under Colonial Mining Code Section 12. It's legal. It's mine." "For the moment." Voss taps a control. The screen shifts to show a cascade of legal filings, military deployment orders, encrypted traffic intercepts. "The Code was written when Azra was a laboratory curiosity. It did not anticipate this. There are already proposals in the Earth Assembly to nationalize all Azra deposits under a joint planetary authority. There are quieter proposals, circulating in corporate boardrooms, to simply... acquire the land by other means. Indenture default. Safety violations. Tragic accidents. You are a first-generation settler, Mr. Neegan. You have no powerful patrons. Your labor force is three free contractors and a pair of aging auto-diggers. You are alone." Arthur feels the drone of the station's own air systems now, a higher, cleaner note than the tug's. "And Antares? What does the Diplomatic Corps want?" "Stability," Voss says simply. "Or rather, a managed instability. We represent interests that prefer the expansion of human civilization not be held hostage to any single corporate monopoly or nationalist cabal. Azra in the wrong hands—or too many hands—could ignite a war that would leave the inner system a radioactive cinder before the first colony ships even reach Alpha Centauri. We propose a partnership." He slides a thin data slate across the table. Arthur does not touch it yet. "In exchange for exclusive development rights under Antares oversight," Voss continues, "you will receive full technical support, a security detachment sufficient to discourage... competitors, and a personal equity stake that would make you one of the wealthiest individuals in the system. Your claim remains yours in name. The vein is developed rationally, the metal allocated according to a carefully balanced formula among the major powers. No one gets everything. No one is left with nothing. The arms race is... moderated." Arthur studies the man's face. "And if I refuse?" Voss's smile does not reach his eyes. "Then the flurry of interest you have already experienced becomes a storm. The retinue that collected you was polite. The next may not be. There are parties who would prefer the engineer who discovered the lode simply... disappear, so that the claim can be reassigned cleanly. There are others who would keep you alive but under far less congenial circumstances—indentured, perhaps, on your own land. The political tug-of-war has already begun, Mr. Neegan. You are the rope." Silence stretches. Through another viewport Arthur sees a distant flicker—perhaps a drive test, a brief blue spear of Cherenkov radiation as some experimental Azra core lights. The future is out there, accelerating. He thinks of the quiet evenings in his dome, the red hills, the slow patient work of extraction. He thinks of the secrets still locked inside the metal: why it catalyzes decay so efficiently, whether the effect can be reversed, whether it can be weaponized beyond the crude devices already rumored. "You paint a grim picture," Arthur says at last. "I paint an accurate one." Voss leans forward. "The offer is generous. It is also temporary. Decide soon. The next transfer window closes in six hours. After that, other interested parties will know you are no longer under our... hospitality." Arthur reaches for the slate. The screen lights with dense contractual language, clauses within clauses, the fine print of empire. Outside, the stars wait, cold and patient. Inside, the air filtration hums on, scrubbing the recycled atmosphere clean of every impurity except ambition. He begins to read.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical competence in capturing Asimov's style and meeting the prompt requirements. The author successfully creates a retrofuturistic atmosphere with detailed world-building that operates on both macro and micro levels - from the geopolitical implications of Azra mining to the specific hum of air filtration systems. The dialogue between Arthur and Voss is well-crafted, revealing character motivations and advancing plot while maintaining tension. The writing effectively establishes stakes through Voss's exposition about corporate interests, government intervention, and the strategic importance of Azra. The political intrigue feels authentic to classic sci-fi, with the Antares Diplomatic Corps serving as a believable third-party manipulator. Arthur's characterization as an overwhelmed but thoughtful engineer rings true, and his reactions feel genuine given the circumstances. However, the piece suffers from some structural issues. The opening section, while atmospheric, takes considerable time to reach the central dialogue scene. The exposition, though necessary for world-building, occasionally feels heavy-handed, particularly in Voss's lengthy explanations of the political situation. Some descriptions veer toward purple prose ("blue spear of Cherenkov radiation") without adding substantial meaning. The dialogue itself is functional but lacks the sharp wit or philosophical depth that characterizes Asimov's best work. Voss comes across as somewhat one-dimensional - the archetypal smooth manipulator - while Arthur remains largely reactive throughout the scene. The ending, with Arthur beginning to read the contract, feels appropriate but lacks dramatic punch. The piece succeeds in creating atmosphere and advancing plot, but doesn't quite achieve the intellectual elegance or character complexity that would elevate it to exceptional quality. It reads as competent genre fiction that captures the surface elements of Asimov's style without fully embodying his deeper storytelling strengths. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 13 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 14 Emotionally Engaging: 14 Emotionally Complex: 12 Coherent: 17 Meandering: 8 Weak Dialogue: 9 Tell-Don't-Show: 10 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 11 Amateurish: 7 Purple Prose: 9 Overwrought: 8 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 15 Overall Impression: 15

+ Contemporary romance: Darkroom Revelations — Score: 83.8
Prompt:
Darkroom Revelations Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of Dani, a 25-year-old assertive art student known for her bold style and take-charge attitude in relationships. For the past semester, she's been quietly fascinated by Elliot (he/him), a 19-year-old boy in her experimental photography class. Dani has been intrigued by his unconventional masculinity: killer thrift-shop-nu-goth style, soft demeanour and quiet intellect. When they're paired for a final project requiring late nights in the darkroom, Elliot tentatively shares that he's been questioning his gender identity and expression. Set during one of these intimate darkroom sessions where the red light creates a confessional atmosphere. Explore Dani's complex attraction—her usual assertiveness complicated by her desire to protect Elliot's vulnerability, her excitement at witnessing his self-discovery, and her confusion about what this means for her own identity as someone typically attracted to more defined gender presentations. The narrative should capture the tender awkwardness of their conversations, Dani's internal struggle between taking charge and wanting to give Elliot agency, and the unexpected chemistry that develops as both find themselves in uncharted territory. 1000 words.
Model Output:
**Darkroom Revelations** The red light in the darkroom always feels like a secret I get to keep. It turns everything the color of old blood and velvet, softens the edges of the world until even the chemical trays look intimate. Tonight the air is thick with the vinegar sting of stop bath and the faint ozone of the enlargers, and Elliot is standing so close that I can smell the thrift-store musk of his black lace shirt mixed with the cheap patchouli he probably found in the same bin. He’s nineteen, five years younger than me, and for the entire semester I’ve been watching him like a camera that can’t stop focusing: the way his soft mouth moves when he talks about Diane Arbus, the deliberate mess of his nu-goth thrift finds—fishnet under a ripped Bauhaus tee, silver rings on long fingers that never quite settle—the quiet intellect that makes him pause before he speaks, as if every word has to pass inspection. I am not usually patient. I take charge. I decide. In relationships I set the tempo, pull people in with the same boldness I bring to a composition. But Elliot makes me hesitate. He’s been questioning things—gender identity, expression—and he told me that three nights ago, voice barely above the hum of the safelight, eyes fixed on a test strip like it might answer for him. “I don’t know if I’m a boy the way everyone assumes,” he said. “Or if I want to be. The clothes help. Looking like this helps. But sometimes I feel like I’m still translating.” I wanted to kiss the uncertainty off his mouth and also wrap him in something soft and tell him there was no rush. That contradiction is still living under my ribs. Tonight we’re printing the final series for our collaborative project: self-portraits that bleed into each other, bodies half-erased, identities overlapping under long exposures. The assignment is supposed to be about “fluidity and the gaze.” I keep thinking the professors have no idea how close to the bone it is. Elliot is loading a sheet of paper into the easel when his elbow knocks the stack I left too near the edge. The whole pile slides, white rectangles flashing under the red light for a fatal second before they hit the floor. “Shit—Dani, I’m so sorry—” His voice cracks on my name. He drops to his knees, scooping useless paper, and I can see the flush rising under the pale powder he dusted across his cheekbones. “I ruined them. All of them.” I crouch with him. My first instinct is pure take-charge: *It’s fine, I’ll run to the supply closet, I know the code, I’ll fix it.* My hand is already halfway to his wrist when I stop myself. He needs to feel the agency he keeps saying he doesn’t trust he has. So I let my fingers hover, then settle lightly on the back of his hand instead of gripping. “Hey. Look at me.” I keep my voice low, the way the red light seems to demand confessions. “Paper is paper. We have more in my bag—unopened, safe. You didn’t ruin anything that matters.” He lifts his head. In this light his eyes are black and huge, the soft demeanour cracking open to something rawer. “I keep messing things up when I’m near you. Not just the paper. The talking. I start saying things I haven’t even said to myself yet.” The chemistry between us has been building like a latent image—invisible until the right moment. Now it’s developing fast. I feel it in the way my pulse jumps when his rings brush my knuckles. I am usually the one who crowds space, who decides the next move. Tonight I want to give him the darkroom, the silence, the choice. “Tell me one of those things,” I say. “If you want. No pressure. Just… I’m here under the same red light.” He sits back on his heels, ruined paper forgotten around us like shed skin. “I like how you look at me. Not like I’m a project or a phase. Like I’m… interesting even when I don’t have the words yet. And I keep wondering if you’d still want to be this close if I woke up tomorrow and wanted different pronouns, or different clothes, or no clothes that signal anything at all.” His laugh is small, embarrassed. “That’s a lot. Sorry.” My own confusion surges up, hot and honest. I’ve always been drawn to more defined presentations—men who knew their lines, women who claimed theirs without hesitation. Elliot’s soft edges, his thrifted armor of lace and eyeliner and quiet questions, should scramble the map I thought I had of my own desire. Instead it redraws it in real time. I am excited—genuinely, bodily excited—to watch him discover himself. The protectiveness is almost overwhelming; I want to stand between him and every clumsy assumption the world will throw. At the same time I am aching to close the last inches, to feel the unexpected heat of him answering me. “I don’t need you finished,” I tell him. The words come out rougher than I intend, my usual assertiveness leaking through. I soften it. “I like the version of you that’s still asking. It makes me ask myself things I thought were settled. Like maybe my type was never as fixed as I pretended. Maybe I just hadn’t met anyone who made the questions feel this good.” He stares at me. The enlarger timer ticks somewhere in the red gloom, marking seconds that feel stretched. Then he reaches out—tentative, giving me every chance to pull away—and rests his fingers against the side of my neck, right over the pulse. The touch is light, almost reverent, and it undoes me more thoroughly than any confident grab ever has. “I don’t know how to do this,” he whispers. “Any of it. Being wanted while I’m still… becoming.” I cover his hand with mine, not trapping, just meeting. “Then we do it the way we make prints. Slow. Test strips first. We can stop whenever the image doesn’t feel right. You set the exposure. I’ll follow your lead tonight.” The offer costs me something—my need to direct, to claim—but the look that breaks across his face is worth every surrendered inch. Something like relief, something like hunger. He leans in first. The kiss is awkward in the best way: too careful, then suddenly not careful enough, the taste of coffee and nerves, the brush of his soft mouth learning pressure against mine. My free hand finds the torn edge of his shirt, not yanking, just anchoring. Heat builds low in my stomach, complicated and bright. I want to push him back against the counter and take, and I want to stay exactly here, letting him decide how deep the next breath goes. When we break apart, breathing the same chemical air, he rests his forehead against mine. “The paper,” he says, half-laughing. “We still need to print.” “We do.” I smile, feeling bold again but in a new register—bold enough to wait. “There’s more in my bag. Unexposed. Fresh start. You load the easel this time. I’ll watch. Tell me what you see when the image comes up.” He nods, gathers himself, and we rise together. The ruined stack stays on the floor, a small white accident that somehow opened the door. Under the red light we keep working—his hands steadier now, my usual commands turned into questions, both of us developing something neither of us planned. The chemistry is no longer latent. It’s fixed, for now, in silver and light and the quiet permission we keep offering each other in the dark. (Word count: 998)
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical craft and emotional intelligence in handling a delicate subject matter. The author successfully creates an intimate, atmospheric setting using the darkroom as both literal space and metaphor for development and revelation. The red light motif is particularly effective, creating a confessional atmosphere that justifies the characters' vulnerability. The characterization of Dani is well-executed, showing her internal conflict between her natural assertiveness and her desire to give Elliot agency. Her voice feels authentic for a 25-year-old art student - confident but not arrogant, sexually aware but capable of growth. The author skillfully shows her attraction evolving in real-time as she encounters something outside her usual preferences. Elliot's portrayal is handled with sensitivity, avoiding stereotypes while capturing genuine uncertainty about gender identity. The dialogue feels natural and age-appropriate, particularly his tentative confession and Dani's measured responses. The power dynamics are thoughtfully navigated - Dani's usual dominance is complicated by Elliot's vulnerability, creating realistic tension. The photography metaphors are well-integrated throughout, from "watching him like a camera that can't stop focusing" to the final image of "developing something neither of us planned." These feel organic rather than forced, enhancing rather than overwhelming the narrative. The pacing builds effectively from observation to confession to physical intimacy, with the spilled paper serving as a well-timed catalyst. The kiss scene strikes an appropriate balance between awkwardness and heat, feeling true to both characters' emotional states. Some minor weaknesses include occasional overwrought phrases ("living under my ribs," "undoes me more thoroughly") and a few moments where the prose becomes slightly purple. The ending, while sweet, borders on being too neat given the complexity of the issues raised. However, the author earns this lightness through careful character development and the specific context of this single encounter. The piece successfully fulfills the prompt's requirements while maintaining literary quality and emotional authenticity. It handles LGBTQ+ themes with respect and nuance, avoiding both fetishization and oversimplification. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 18 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Emotionally Complex: 17 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 6 Overwrought: 5 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 4 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17
